title: Clonal hematopoiesis is associated with distinct rheumatoid arthritis phenotypes
research_question: Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) is associated with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and its subtypes, and whether specific CHIP mutations correlate with distinct RA phenotypes and outcomes.
methods_summary: Whole-exome sequencing (WES) and SNP array analysis in FINRISK cohort (n=10,089) to detect CHIP, lymphoid driver variants, and mosaic chromosomal alterations (mCAs).
SNP array-based CHIP hotspot annotation in FinnGen cohort (n=520,210) with manual curation of intensity plots.
Targeted next-generation sequencing (NGS) of 65 myeloid genes in 573 newly diagnosed, untreated RA patients and 163 healthy controls.
Multivariable logistic regression and Cox proportional hazards models adjusted for age, sex, smoking, and genetic ancestry.
Comparison of CHIP prevalence and clinical parameters (CRP, ESR, DAS28, HAQ, seropositivity) across RA subtypes.
dataset: FINRISK study (n=10,089 with WES), FinnGen cohort (n=520,210 with SNP array), and a cohort of 573 newly diagnosed RA patients from four Finnish hospitals.
key_findings: CHIP was associated with prevalent RA in FINRISK (OR 2.06, P=0.029) and FinnGen (OR 1.49, P<0.001).
In FinnGen, CHIP was associated with inferior overall survival in participants with RA (HR 1.92, P=0.011).
In newly diagnosed RA, DNMT3A-mutated seropositive patients had higher inflammatory markers and disease activity.
TET2 mutations were enriched in seronegative RA (P=0.009).
Non-DNMT3A CHIP was associated with seronegative RA in FinnGen (OR 3.60, P<0.001).
extracted_insights: CHIP, particularly DNMT3A and TET2 mutations, shows context-dependent associations with RA subtypes: DNMT3A linked to more severe seropositive RA, while TET2 enriched in seronegative RA, suggesting potential for personalized anti-inflammatory therapy (e.g., IL-1β inhibition) based on CHIP status.
